perturbation.original_text: Payment terms are net 30 days after the date of the invoice, or the date of receipt by Liquidmetal of the invoice if the invoice is received more than 10 days after the date of the invoice.
perturbation.changed_text: Payment terms are net 90 days after the date of the invoice, or the date of receipt by Liquidmetal of the invoice if the invoice is received more than 10 days after the date of the invoice.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to net 90 days could violate the California Prompt Payment Statute, which mandates specific payment timelines for businesses, particularly for smaller businesses. A longer payment period may be deemed unreasonable and contradict the statute's intent.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Prompt Payment Statute - Cal. Civ. Code §§ 3300-3326
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 3320 states that if a public works contract fails to specify the time for payment, the contractor may request payment within 10 days of completion. By extending the payment timeframe to 90 days, the revised text would contravene this legal standard, as a longer payment period may be deemed unreasonable and contradict the statute's intent, especially if Liquidmetal is considered a larger entity imposing terms on a smaller one.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. COMPENSATORY RELIEF [3281 - 3361] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Measure of Damages [[3300.] - 3361] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 1. Damages for Breach of Contract [[3300.] - 3322] ( Article 1 enacted 1872. ) 3320. (a) In each contract for public works of improvement, entered into on or after January 1, 1996, the public agency shall pay to the prime design professional any progress payment within 30 days of receipt of a written demand for payment in accordance with the contract, and the final retention payment within 45 days of receipt of a written demand for payment in accordance with the contract. If the public agency disputes in good faith any portion of the amount due, it may withhold from the payment an amount not to exceed 150 percent of the disputed amount. The disputed amount withheld is not subject to any penalty authorized by this section. (b) If any amount is wrongfully withheld or is not timely paid in violation of this section, the prime design professional shall be entitled to a penalty of 1 1 / 2 percent for the improperly withheld amount, in lieu o
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies payment terms as net 30 days, while the changed text extends this to net 90 days. The Perturbation Explanation states this change could violate the California Prompt Payment Statute. The Law Explanation also mentions that extending the payment timeframe to 90 days would contravene legal standards. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code Section 3320, discusses payment timelines in public works contracts. Although the snippet focuses on public works, it supports the idea that California law regulates payment timelines. The change directly contradicts the principle of prompt payment potentially required or expected under California law, making it a direct contradiction because it extends the payment period significantly.

perturbation.original_text: Unless Liquidmetal notifies Eutectix that the Liquidmetal Product does not meet the Specifications within thirty (30) calendar days after receipt of the Liquidmetal Product, then the Liquidmetal Product shall be deemed Accepted.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless Liquidmetal notifies Eutectix that the Liquidmetal Product does not meet the Specifications within ten (10) calendar days after receipt of the Liquidmetal Product, then the Liquidmetal Product shall be deemed Accepted.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the acceptance notification period to 10 days might be considered an unreasonably short time frame under the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC), particularly if thorough inspection requires more time. This could contradict UCC provisions requiring a reasonable opportunity for inspection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-606 - What Constitutes Acceptance of Goods
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-606 defines acceptance and implies a reasonable opportunity for inspection. By reducing the notification timeframe to 10 days, the revised text potentially deprives Liquidmetal of a reasonable time to inspect the goods thoroughly, especially if complex specifications are involved, thus contradicting the UCC's implied provision for reasonable inspection periods prior to acceptance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. View Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Article 4, Bank Deposits and Collections Uniform Commercial Code Article 4 governs bank deposits and collecti
perturbation.justification: The altered contract text reduces the time for Liquidmetal to inspect the product to 10 days. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest that this timeframe might be too short to allow for 'reasonable' inspection, which violates the UCC's implied provision for reasonable inspection periods. The provided text indicates that UCC Article 2 governs the sale of goods. Although neither scraped snippet directly states that a 'reasonable' time for inspection must be allowed, the Law Explanation directly indicates a contradiction between the reduced time frame and a reasonable time as per the UCC. Given the provided information, the shortened period conflicts with the UCC's intention.

perturbation.original_text: Upon reasonable prior notice to Eutectix and at Liquidmetal's expense, no more than once per year, during the Term of this Agreement and for one (1) year following the expiration or termination of this Agreement, Liquidmetal or its designee shall have the right from time to time to confirm and validate:
perturbation.changed_text: Upon reasonable prior notice to Eutectix and at Liquidmetal's expense, no more than once per year, during the Term of this Agreement and for five (5) years following the expiration or termination of this Agreement, Liquidmetal or its designee shall have the right from time to time to confirm and validate:
perturbation.explanation: Extending the audit rights to 5 years post-termination could be seen as an unreasonable burden, especially if the relevant information should be reasonably available within a much shorter period and might conflict with California statutes of limitations for certain contract disputes. The relevant statute of limitations period for breach of written contract in California is four years.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure § 337 - Statute of Limitations
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 337 sets a four-year statute of limitations for actions based on written contracts. Extending the audit period to five years post-termination grants Liquidmetal a right to audit records beyond the statutory period for bringing claims related to the contract, which may be seen as an unreasonable or unenforceable extension of contractual rights potentially exceeding the statute of limitations period.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. OF THE TIME OF COMMENCING CIVIL ACTIONS [312 - 366.3] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. The Time of Commencing Actions Other Than for the Recovery of Real Property [335 - 349.4] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 337. Within four years: (a) An action upon any contract, obligation or liability founded upon an instrument in writing, except as provided in Section 336a; provided, that the time within which any action for a money judgment for the balance due upon an obligation for the payment of which a deed of trust or mortgage with power of sale upon real property or any interest therein was given as security, following the exercise of the power of sale in such deed of trust or mortgage, may be brought shall not extend beyond three months after the time of sale under such deed of trust or mortgage. (b) An action to recover (1) upon a book account whether consisting of one or more entries; (2) upon an account stated based upon an account in writing, but the acknowledgment of the account stated need not be in writing; (3) a balance due upon a mutual, open and current account, the items of which are in writing; provided, however, that if an account stated is based upon an
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the audit rights to 5 years post-termination, while California Code of Civil Procedure Section 337 sets a four-year statute of limitations for actions based on written contracts. The extension grants Liquidmetal a right to audit records beyond the statutory period for bringing claims, making the contractual terms potentially unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that CCP 337 details that actions upon a contract in writing have a four year limit.
